original_abstract:
We address the challenge of embedding large-scale knowledge graphs into continuous vector spaces. While the recently proposed TransE method has gained traction due to its efficiency and state-of-the-art performance, it struggles to accurately model complex relational properties such as reflexivity and one-to-many, many-to-one, and many-to-many mappings. While more sophisticated models can capture these nuances, they often do so at the cost of significant computational overhead.

To bridge this gap between model capacity and efficiency, we introduce TransH (Translation on Hyperplanes). This approach represents each relation as a specific hyperplane, where entities are projected before undergoing a translation operation. By allowing entities to take on different representations depending on the relation involved, TransH effectively preserves complex mapping properties while maintaining a model complexity comparable to TransE.

Furthermore, we address the practical issue of incomplete knowledge graphs by refining the training process. We propose a simple yet effective sampling "trick" that utilizes a relation's mapping properties to construct negative examples more intelligently, thereby reducing the prevalence of false negative labels. We evaluate our approach through extensive experiments on link prediction, triplet classification, and fact extraction using benchmark datasets such as WordNet and Freebase. Our results demonstrate that TransH achieves significant improvements in predictive accuracy over TransE while remaining highly scalable for large-scale applications.
humanized_abstract:
Embedding a large-scale knowledge graph into a continuous vector space is an important topic in the field of knowledge graph representation. Although the newly proposed TransE achieves the best results so far, the model has limited ability in dealing with complex relational properties such as reflexive, one-to-many, many-to-one and many-to-many relations. More advanced models could make up for the shortage but at the price of slowing down.

It is difficult for many models to achieve the required efficiency in terms of computing resource, especially when the models are extremely powerful, like TransR and TransD. Therefore, this research adopts a method named Translation on Hyperplanes (TransH) to improve the computational efficiency. For this, every relation is denoted as the vector of a hyperplane. Every entity in the model needs to be translated into the corresponding hyperplane to get the corresponding vector in that hyperplane. Then a translation operation can be adopted in the corresponding hyperplane to get the relation vector. By doing so, entities can achieve different mapping capabilities in TransH to keep the complex mapping abilities, and meanwhile the complexity of the model is reduced to the scale of TransE.

This paper extends TransE to tackle not only the theoretical challenges caused by the simplified structure of embeddings, but also the practical issues caused by the incomplete nature of knowledge graphs. Specifically, we propose a way to refine the learning procedure, with a simple sampling “trick” that turns the mappings of relations to select more adequate negative samples (with less false negative labels). We perform thorough experiments on link prediction, triplet classification and fact extraction tasks, which utilize WordNet and Freebase as knowledge bases. Our evaluation results show that, TransH enhances the accuracy of models built upon the foundation of TransE and even exceeds the baseline remarkably, meanwhile remains good scalability for large knowledge graphs.

document.output:

Although the newly proposed TransE achieves the best results so far, the model has limited ability in dealing with complex relational properties such as reflexive, one-to-many, many-to-one and many-to-many relations. More advanced models could make up for the shortage but at the price of slowing down.

Every entity in the model needs to be translated into the corresponding hyperplane to get the corresponding vector in that hyperplane. By doing so, entities can achieve different mapping capabilities in TransH to keep the complex mapping abilities, and meanwhile the complexity of the model is reduced to the scale of TransE.

This paper extends TransE to tackle not only the theoretical challenges caused by the simplified structure of embeddings, but also the practical issues caused by the incomplete nature of knowledge graphs. document.input:
We address the challenge of embedding large-scale knowledge graphs into continuous vector spaces. While the recently proposed TransE method has gained traction due to its efficiency and state-of-the-art performance, it struggles to accurately model complex relational properties such as reflexivity and one-to-many, many-to-one, and many-to-many mappings. While more sophisticated models can capture these nuances, they often do so at the cost of significant computational overhead.

This approach represents each relation as a specific hyperplane, where entities are projected before undergoing a translation operation. Furthermore, we address the practical issue of incomplete knowledge graphs by refining the training process. Our results demonstrate that TransH achieves significant improvements in predictive accuracy over TransE while remaining highly scalable for large-scale applications.
